steps.text: Prepare the diluted test chemical from a reagent-grade product per the preparation sheets provided by the Study Director.
Use HACH Test Kits to verify available chlorine for diluted samples.
Perform an analysis of formulation chemistry on each lot (one container) by OPP chemists to confirm the percent active ingredient.
Initiate a new stock culture of Bacillus subtilis (ATCC # 19659).
Obtain Study Director approval if using an existing stock culture that meets quality control standards.
Provide inoculated porcelain carriers to each laboratory per the modified methodology for AOAC Method 966.04.
Follow the revised method for generation of spore suspensions and inoculation of carriers.
Determine carrier counts and HCl resistance, ensuring they meet AOAC method specifications, in advance of shipping the carriers.
Produce spores for use in the TSM per the method provided.
Inoculate carriers from one spore preparation per laboratory.
Ensure enough carriers are inoculated to perform the entire study (approx. 150).
Perform sodium hypochlorite test method.
Perform peracetic acid/hydrogen peroxide test method.
Perform glutaraldehyde test method.
Initiate B. subtilis culture using nutrient broth to rehydrate a lyophilized culture, and incubate the broth culture for 24 ± 2 hours at 36 ± 1ºC prior to streak inoculation.
Streak inoculate a set of six nutrient agar slopes and incubate for 24 ± 2 hours at 36 ± 1ºC.
Perform purity and identification confirmation testing for QC using colony morphology on TSA, Gram stain, or other identification systems.
Store at 2-5ºC after incubation.
Maintain stock culture on nutrient agar slants by monthly transfers (30 ± 2 days).
Using growth from a stock culture tube, inoculate 10 mL tubes of nutrient broth and incubate tubes for 24 ± 2 hours on an orbital shaker at approximately 150 rpm at 36 ± 1ºC.
Use this culture to inoculate amended nutrient agar plates.
Inoculate each plate with 500 µl of broth culture and spread the inoculum with a sterile bent glass rod or suitable spreading device.
Wrap each plate with parafilm or place in plastic bags.
Incubate plates inverted for 12-14 days at 36 ± 1ºC.
Harvest the spores by adding 10 mL cold sterile water to each plate.
Using a spreader, remove growth from plates and pipet suspensions into 15 mL sterile conical tubes.
Centrifuge tubes at 5000 rpm for approximately 10 minutes at room temperature.
Remove and discard supernatant.
Resuspend pellet.
Remove fraction C tubes after 30 ± 2 min rotation/incubation from incubator.
Add 600 µL ice-cold LB Broth to each tube.
Proceed to dilution and plating if another analyst is available, or store fraction C tubes in refrigerator.
Vortex mix each microcentrifuge tube thoroughly prior to making dilutions.
For each fraction and control tube, remove 100 µL and serially dilute 10-fold in 900 μL ice-cold LB broth.
For each carrier, direct plate 100 µL of the sufficient dilutions onto TSA or NA to ensure obtaining counts within the target range of 30-300 CFU/plate.
Incubate plates a minimum of 24 ± 2 hr at 36 ± 1ºC.
Record control counts at 24 ± 2 hr.
Record treated carrier counts at 24 ± 2 hr and at 48 ± 2 hr.

Confirm the identity of a minimum of one representative colony taken from at least one plate per treatment level (if available) using Gram staining, general growth media (e.g., TSA or NA) or other confirmation procedure.
Use counts which fall within 0-300 CFU/plate for calculations.
Obtain the total number of spores.
Label fraction A, B and C microcentrifuge tubes with fraction letter (e.g. A, B, or C) and carrier number (e.g., 1, 2, 3, etc.).

In advance of testing, prepare fraction B and fraction C microcentrifuge tubes: Add 400 μL of ice-cold sterile water to fraction B tubes. Add 400 μL of ice-cold LB broth to fraction C tubes. Store these tubes in a refrigerator (2-5°C) until ready for use.
Label dilution microcentrifuge tubes (serial dilution blanks) with fraction letter (e.g. A, B, or C), carrier number (e.g. 1, 2, 3, etc.), and dilution (e.g., 10-1, 10-2, 10-3, etc.).
Prepare serial dilution blanks for A, B, and C fractions for all test carriers.
Design PCR assay primers and probes.
Choose the genomic region to be targeted by the assay.
Predict the specificity and sensitivity of the primers and/or probe.

constraints.text: Personal protective clothing or devices are recommended during the handling of these items for purpose of activation, dilution, or efficacy testing.
A chemical fume hood or other containment equipment may be employed when appropriate during performing tasks with concentrated products.

These microbiological methods are very technique sensitive and technique-oriented, thus exact adherence to the method, good laboratory practices, and quality control (QC) are required for proficiency and validity of the results.
Detergents used in washing glassware may leave residues which are bacteriostatic. Test for inhibitory residues on glassware periodically.
